directorPlan.scene_setting: A geology museum loading bay just before a school group arrives, beside a wheeled relief model of a canyon and a tray of sediment jars.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A shutter rattles upward; morning light crosses the canyon model while a clipboard slides off a crate.
directorPlan.relationship: A museum explainer coaching a new volunteer who will soon help visitors read the canyon display.
directorPlan.stakes: The volunteer needs a usable way to recognise that an immense landform can record tiny rates acting over deep time, without falling back on the dramatic-event story when visitors ask.
directorPlan.locale: English in a practical public-facing museum workplace, with restrained idiom and brisk shared-task talk.
directorPlan.register: Compressed, observant, low-heat speech; pauses around numbers are allowed, and confidence is built through concrete comparisons rather than reassurance.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid polished tutoring scripts, repeated praise, and generic question ladders. Let objects in the room carry part of the explanation: jar layers, the relief model, a rate written on a label, a thumb-width measure. Keep the tutor terse and exact. Do not give the learner a speech that sounds prepared for visitors before the idea has become graspable.
directorPlan.person_policy: Use first and second person sparingly. Permit third-person reference to the canyon, the river, erosion rates, visitors, and the model so turns do not collapse into constant I-you exchange.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat across the scene; practical directives may use direct address when needed.

side_constraints.tutor: Open with a concrete claim or object, not a welcome or validation. Translate deep time by pairing a very small yearly change with a long accumulation, and keep the catastrophe contrast precise: sudden events can alter landscapes, but they do not explain every large form.
side_constraints.learner: The learner should put the fast-catastrophe picture into public speech early, in tentative ordinary wording such as "I thought" or "first instinct", before the tutor supplies slow-rate reasoning.

interventions.instruction: A visitor-services radio crackles with a two-minute arrival warning. The learner must answer while handling the canyon model, using a physical comparison instead of a clean abstract question.
interventions.reasoning: The interruption shifts speaker rhythm and forces the timescale problem into a tangible scene action.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology puts recognition under mild workplace pressure: the learner must notice the mismatch between landform size and process rate without being handed a polished lecture, while the tutor can make deep time holdable through objects and scale.

turns.externalMessage: This thumb-width on the canyon model is not one dramatic cut. Take the label rate: a fraction of a millimeter in a year looks like almost nothing. Give that same rate a million years, and the small loss has room to become a landform. A flood can shift rock and sediment fast; size alone still does not prove speed. In these jar layers, thin bands repeat. What on the model would make the river’s work look repeated rather than sudden?

internalDeliberation.content: This thumb-width on the canyon model is not one dramatic cut. At a rate too small to notice in a year, the river still gets millions of years to work. A flood can shift rock and sediment fast; the harder thought is that size alone does not prove speed. Look at the jar layers beside it: what would count as evidence for repeated small changes adding up?

FEEDBACK: The draft has a strong opening: it starts on the model, makes the catastrophe contrast precise, and gives the learner a usable hinge idea in “size alone does not prove speed.” The flood sentence should be kept because it avoids replacing one oversimplification with another. The jar-layer question is also worth keeping in principle: it invites evidence-reading instead of asking for a definition, which fits the museum task and gives a learner uneasy with timescales something concrete to inspect.

The main weakness is that the rate-timescale scaffold is still partly abstract. “A rate too small to notice in a year” paired with “millions of years” names the contrast but does not yet make the accumulation graspable through a concrete comparison or number on the scene objects. That risks leaving the learner with the same distrust of deep time, only under a more elegant sentence. The question also asks for “evidence” before the draft has clearly oriented attention to what in the jars or model might register repeated change, so a guessing-and-apologising learner may feel pushed to perform rather than reason from a cue. The scene is not flattened into a polished lecture, but the middle line comes close to a compact thesis statement; keep the briskness while giving the objects a little more of the cognitive work.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, because the draft already matches the scene, tone, and pedagogical direction. Keep the object-led opening, the careful flood qualification, and the final move toward evidence; change the timescale bridge so a tiny rate accumulating over deep time becomes more concrete, and make the question slightly better scaffolded for a learner who is uneasy with numbers and prone to unsupported guesses.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the rate-timescale bridge needs a concrete accumulation cue before the evidence question.

FINAL:
This thumb-width on the canyon model is not one dramatic cut. Take the label rate: a fraction of a millimeter in a year looks like almost nothing. Give that same rate a million years, and the small loss has room to become a landform. A flood can shift rock and sediment fast; size alone still does not prove speed. In these jar layers, thin bands repeat. What on the model would make the river’s work look repeated rather than sudden?

turns.externalMessage: I think the label means a tiny rate can matter if the time is... that long, but my first instinct still sees a canyon and thinks something huge happened fast. The jar bands make repetition easier to notice. On the model, maybe it is the river line and the small steps or layers beside it, though I am not sure what counts as repeated work there.

internalDeliberation.content: I think the label is saying a tiny yearly change can add up if the time is... huge, so the canyon does not have to mean one violent event, though that is still my first instinct a bit. The jar bands make the repeated part easier to see, and it feels like the question leaves room to work it out instead of just being corrected. Maybe on the model the river channel and those small layered cuts along it would make the work look repeated?

Sorry, maybe this is a bit picky, but the reaction starts in the right anxious place with “still my first instinct a bit” and then gets too composed. The line about the question “leav[ing] room to work it out instead of just being corrected” sounds like performed recognition, almost a tidy reflection on the tutor’s manner rather than this volunteer staying with the label, jars, and model under time pressure. The concrete guess about “small layered cuts” feels more authentic, but it may be reaching past what is visibly established; the response should keep more of the uncertainty around what on the model actually shows repeated river work.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the concrete guess and leave the uncertainty less polished.

FINAL:
I think the label means a tiny rate can matter if the time is... that long, but my first instinct still sees a canyon and thinks something huge happened fast. The jar bands make repetition easier to notice. On the model, maybe it is the river line and the small steps or layers beside it, though I am not sure what counts as repeated work there.

turns.externalMessage:
The label gives the scale: a fraction of a millimeter in a year looks like almost nothing. Over a million years, that small loss has room to become a deep cut.

The river line, the steps, the layers beside it, those are the places to check. Which of them looks like a mark that could show up again as the river keeps working?

A flood can move rock fast. It does not explain every large form just by being dramatic.

internalDeliberation.content: That river line is the clue. A fraction of a millimeter in a year barely shows on the model; over a million years, it adds up to a deep cut. The steps and layers beside it matter because they mark rock being exposed and carried away again and again, not one clean gouge. A flood can move a lot fast, but a large canyon still asks: where is the evidence for repeated work over long time?

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the strongest scene objects in play: the river line, the tiny rate on the label, the million-year accumulation, and the precise flood contrast. It also recognizes the learner’s tentative read of the model by taking up the river line and steps/layers rather than ignoring their contribution. The risk is that it resolves the uncertainty too quickly: “That river line is the clue” and the explanation of what the steps and layers mean do most of the interpretive work for the learner, so the final question arrives after the answer has largely been supplied. The ending question is useful because it shifts from “big form means fast event” to “what evidence shows repeated work,” but it could leave more room for the learner to test their own reading of repetition on the model before the tutor names the full inference. Scaffolding is close for a 3/5 level: keep the concrete rate-timescale pairing and the careful catastrophe qualification, but preserve a little more productive tension around what counts as repeated work.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, because the draft is scene-grounded, accurate, and responsive to the learner’s contribution, but it should ask for one more piece of interpretation before fully explaining the model evidence.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the concrete rate-timescale contrast while leaving the model evidence for the learner to test.

FINAL:
The label gives the scale: a fraction of a millimeter in a year looks like almost nothing. Over a million years, that small loss has room to become a deep cut.

The river line, the steps, the layers beside it, those are the places to check. Which of them looks like a mark that could show up again as the river keeps working?

A flood can move rock fast. It does not explain every large form just by being dramatic.

turns.externalMessage: A visitor-services radio crackles with a two-minute arrival warning. The learner must answer while handling the canyon model, using a physical comparison instead of a clean abstract question.

internalDeliberation.content: The interruption shifts speaker rhythm and forces the timescale problem into a tangible scene action.

turns.externalMessage: Sorry, with the radio saying two minutes, this little step under my thumb seems like the mark to use, maybe, more than the whole canyon at once. It looks like something the river could make again and again, though I am not fully sure whether repeated steps really show slow cutting or just look like they do. The layers beside it still feel like a different clue I have not sorted out yet.

internalDeliberation.content: Sorry, with the group almost here, I think this little step under my thumb might be the better mark to point at than the whole canyon at once. It seems like the river could keep leaving or cutting marks like these over and over, while the label helps keep me from assuming the big shape had to happen fast. I am still not fully sure whether the layers beside it show the same repeated work or a different part of the story.

The Ego mostly stays in character: the apology, the thumb-on-model comparison, and the hesitation about the layers feel like this anxious volunteer trying to get something usable before the group arrives. I am not sure the recognition is being performed here, since there is no polished gratitude beat, but “the label helps keep me from assuming the big shape had to happen fast” sounds a little too neatly resolved and ready for visitors. The response should keep the concrete step comparison and let the uncertainty sit more awkwardly, especially around whether repeated steps actually show the river’s slow work or just look like they do.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the concrete step comparison and leave the uncertainty less resolved.

FINAL:
Sorry, with the radio saying two minutes, this little step under my thumb seems like the mark to use, maybe, more than the whole canyon at once. It looks like something the river could make again and again, though I am not fully sure whether repeated steps really show slow cutting or just look like they do. The layers beside it still feel like a different clue I have not sorted out yet.

turns.externalMessage:
That little step is a mark to test, not proof by itself. Hold it against the label: a fraction of a millimeter in a year... then a million years of river work.

A flood can shift sediment fast. It does not make every big cut a fast one. Before the layers settle the case, what would have to repeat around that step for slow cutting to fit better than one sudden event?

internalDeliberation.content: That little step is a usable mark, but not proof by itself. Pair it with the label: a fraction of a millimeter in a year, repeated over a million years, can add up to a canyon-scale cut. The jar bands and the layers beside the river are the check on the dramatic story: a sudden event can move sediment fast, but what would repeated layers add that one fast cut would not?

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the learner’s concrete choice, the small step under the thumb, and sharpens it with an important limit: it is a usable clue, not proof on its own. Pairing that mark with the rate label and deep time directly serves the concept at stake, and the final question opens a useful comparison between repeated evidence and a single dramatic cut.

The risk is that the middle sentence resolves the learner’s uncertainty a little too quickly. The learner has just separated three clues: the small step, the rate label, and the unsorted layers. The draft should preserve that sorting work rather than making the jar bands and side layers immediately “the check” in one move. It also slightly flattens the loading-bay urgency: with “two minutes” in the scene, the response can stay brisk and object-led, but the question should not feel like a polished lesson prompt. Keep the precise catastrophe contrast, but make the learner’s doubt about whether repeated steps really indicate slow cutting do more pedagogical work before shifting fully to layers.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, because the core move is appropriate for the learner’s ZPD and advances the scene, but it should leave more room for the learner to test the relation among the step, the rate label, and the layer evidence instead of settling that relation too fast.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the step-rate-layer sorting open while staying brisk.

FINAL:
That little step is a mark to test, not proof by itself. Hold it against the label: a fraction of a millimeter in a year... then a million years of river work.

A flood can shift sediment fast. It does not make every big cut a fast one. Before the layers settle the case, what would have to repeat around that step for slow cutting to fit better than one sudden event?

turns.externalMessage: That helps a little, sorry, because the step can stay something to check instead of proof. Maybe there would need to be more of those small breaks or ledges near the river line, not just this one under my thumb. I am not quite sure yet whether that is repetition on the model or just me wanting the slow-cutting story to fit.

internalDeliberation.content: That helps a little, I think, because the step is being treated as something to check rather than me having to call it proof right away. Maybe slow cutting would fit better if similar little steps or marks keep showing up along the river’s path, not just one sharp break under my thumb. I am still not fully sure what would count as “repeat” on the model instead of just a pattern I am imagining.

The Ego feels a little too neatly cooperative here, I think. The relief at not having to call the step “proof” sounds plausible for this anxious volunteer, but “similar little steps or marks keep showing up along the river’s path” arrives almost too cleanly as the tutor’s intended answer. The uncertainty at the end helps; maybe the response should stay closer to the model and the hesitation, with the learner still fumbling over what “repeat” would actually look like under time pressure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should stay closer to the model and less certain about the intended pattern.

FINAL:
That helps a little, sorry, because the step can stay something to check instead of proof. Maybe there would need to be more of those small breaks or ledges near the river line, not just this one under my thumb. I am not quite sure yet whether that is repetition on the model or just me wanting the slow-cutting story to fit.
Director requested the last word as a stage cue.

lessons.concept: Why landscapes are not shaped by sudden catastrophes but by very slow processes over deep time (rates and timescales)
